coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode detection sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots from pine needles for the determination of Fe3+ and folic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bingying
Guo, Lijun
Yan, Xiangtong
Hou, Faju
Zhong, Linlin
Xu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from pine needles were obtained by one-step hydrothermal synthesis without any chemical reagents. The fluorescence quenching and absorbance enhancement of N-CDs occurred when Fe3+ and folic acid (FA) were added. Based on this, the dual-mode detection sensor by fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry for the determination of Fe3+ and FA was established. Detected by the dual-mode detection sensor under the optimized condition, the linear range of Fe3+ was 0.1–540 μM and FA was 0.1–165 μM. At the same time, the two inputs “NOR” and “OR” logic gates are constructed successfully according to the dual-mode sensor signals. The proposed dual-mode detection sensor is simple, efficient and stable; it can be applied to determinate Fe3+ and FA in practical samples successfully and the results are satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Dual-mode detection sensor
Folic acid (FA)
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-mode sensing mechanism of N-CDs for Fe3+ and FA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image and (B) Size distribution of N-CDs.
(A) FT-IR spectrum, (B) Full-scan XPS spectrum, (C) C 1s XPS, (D) N 1s XPS and (E) O 1s XPS of N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption/fluorescence excitation/ fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs (inset: photographs of N-CDs under UV light (a) and sunlight (b), respectively). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs excited by different wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra and (C) absorption spectra of N-CDs/Fe3+ system. (B) Fluorescence intensity vs. Fe3+ relationship (the inset shows linear detection range). (D) Absorbance vs. Fe3+ relationship.
(A) Fluorescence spectra and (C) absorption spectra of N-CDs/FA system. (B) Fluorescence vs. FA relationship (the inset shows linear detection range). (D) Absorbance vs. FA relationship.
Preparation of N-CDs and its dual-mode sensing mechanism for FA and Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode detection sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots from pine needles for the determination of Fe3+ and folic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Chemistry, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Material Chemistry, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Bingying Wang and Lijun Guo contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of N-CDs and its dual-mode sensing mechanism for Fe3+ and FA.

In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from pine needles were obtained by one-step hydrothermal synthesis without any chemical reagents. The fluorescence quenching and absorbance enhancement of N-CDs occurred when Fe3+ and folic acid (FA) were added. Based on this, the dual-mode detection sensor by fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry for the determination of Fe3+ and FA was established. Detected by the dual-mode detection sensor under the optimized condition, the linear range of Fe3+ was 0.1–540 μM and FA was 0.1–165 μM. At the same time, the two inputs “NOR” and “OR” logic gates are constructed successfully according to the dual-mode sensor signals. The proposed dual-mode detection sensor is simple, efficient and stable; it can be applied to determinate Fe3+ and FA in practical samples successfully and the results are satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA) is absolutely necessary to the growth and reproduction of cells; it can be obtained from foods and pharmaceutical preparations [1,2]. It participates in many metabolic processes, such as the transport of amino acid carbon units and enzyme processing, metabolism of genetic material and protein, cell growth, nucleic acid metabolism, promotion of red blood cell formation and maturation and improvement of immunity [3,4]. FA deficiency has impact on the normal physiological activities and is directly related to neural tube malformation, coronary heart disease, stroke, tumors and other diseases [5,6].

Iron plays an momentous role in organisms. It is an essential component of red blood cells and is a key component of many enzymes [7]. At present, iron deficiency becomes a common nutritional deficiency in the world, and can bring some physiological damage and biological disorders, which probably result in a variety of serious diseases, such as heart disease, kidney failure, liver damage, iron deficiency anemia, and even cancer [8,9]. Most of the time, iron intake comes from foods such as vegetables, fruits, eggs and milk [10].

Quantitative determination of Fe3+ or FA have been reported [11–14], it is still very significative both on theory and on practice to establish a simple, reliable and sensitive approach to determinate Fe3+ and FA simultaneously in the field of food.

Carbon dots (CDs) were first reported by Xu et al in 2004 [15] and have been widely used due to their good biocompatibility and photoluminescence characteristics [16–18]. More interestingly, the doping of non-metallic elements (such as N, S, F, etc.) provides more functional groups and active sites on the surface of CDs, which further expands the application scope [19,20]. Among multitudinous preparation methods, hydrothermal treatment with natural precursors is less time, easy and low cost. The hydrothermal treatment of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from prunus lannesiana, orange juice, carrot juice and their applications have been reported [21–23].

In this paper, extremely economical and green N-CDs with excellent water solubility were developed by one-step hydrothermal synthesis using pine needles as both carbon source and nitrogen source. The prepared N-CDs have high fluorescence emission intensity at 400 nm and absorbance at 283 nm. Further studies showed the fluorescence of N-CDs could be selectively quenched and the absorbance of N-CDs could be selectively enhanced by Fe3+ and FA (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, the dual-mode detection sensor by fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry, and the two inputs “NOR” and “OR” logic gates were developed. The proposed method allows for the detection of Fe3+ and FA in practical food samples with high efficiency and acceptable sensitivity, respectively, so the newly established dual-mode detection sensor has some practical value.
Information about reagent materials and instruments can be found in the supplementary material.

N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. 25 mL ultrapure water and 2 g powdered pine needles were sealed into a stainless autoclave lined with Teflon, heated for 6 h at 200 °C, and then cooled naturally. The obtained solution was centrifuged for 7 min at 12000 rpm to remove large particles and dialyzed in ultrapure water (30 h). Thus N-CDs were obtained, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

To determine the QY of N-CDs, quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4 solutions, QY ∼ 54%) was selected as the criterion reference. Calculation for the QY of N-CDs was
Q Y
x
=
Q Y
r e f
×
I x
/
I
r e f
×
A
r e f
/
A x
×
(
η x
/
η
r e f
)
2

In this equation, “I” means the integral area of the fluorescence peak and “A” denotes the absorbance, “η” refers to the refractive index of solvent. The subscript “x” represents N-CDs; “ref” refers to quinine sulfate.

In a programmed assay, several different final concentrations of Fe3+ (0.1–540 μM) were diluted with 0.1 M buffer solution of Tris-HCl (pH = 7.0) containing 0.027 mg/mL N-CDs to 3.00 mL. Fluorescence intensity and absorbance were recorded after equilibrium for 30 s. In addition, to test the selectivity of the sensor, the same experiment was done with variety of metal ions (540 μM) instead of Fe3+.

For the detection of FA, N-CDs (0.027 mg/mL) and FA (0.1–165 μM) were poured into a cuvette, and Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.0) was adding until 3.00 mL. Then the fluorescence spectra together with UV − vis spectra of the mixture were measured after the reaction for 1 min at ordinary temperature.

For practical samples analysis, some foods (vegetables, eggs, grains and fruits) from the supermarket were taken as the practical samples to evaluate the practicality of the sensor. After a series of pretreatment operations on the foods, Fe3+ and FA were determined by the proposed sensor respectively.
In addition, the standard addition recovery experiments were carried out to test the accuracy of the dual-mode detection sensor.

As indicated in the representative HRTEM image of prepared N-CDs (Fig. 1 A), N-CDs had relatively good dispersion with diameter 3.56 ± 0.85 nm (Fig. 1B). The functional groups of N-CDs were investigated by FTIR, two peaks at 1636 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 (Fig. 2 A) were associated with stretching vibration of C = O bond and – OH/– NH bond respectively [24,25]. The elements of N-CDs were further admeasured by XPS spectroscopy and three different peaks at 285 eV (C 1s), 400 eV (N 1s) and 532 eV (O 1s) were obtained (Fig. 2B). The existence of C – C bond, C = C bond, C – N bond and C-O bond was proved by the four peaks of C1s spectrum at 284.5 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV and 286.9 eV (Fig. 2C), respectively. The two peaks of N1S spectrum correspond to -C-N (399.6 eV) and -N-H (400.1 eV) (Fig. 2D). The three peaks obtained from O 1s spectrum were C = O - C /- COOH (531.2 eV), O = C – OH (532.1 eV) and C - O (533.3 eV) (Fig. 2E) [26–30].

The absorption spectrum of UV–vis (Fig. 3 A) exhibits N-CDs present a clear absorption peak at 283 nm, this π-π* transition phenomenon may be attributed to C = C bond [31]. Furthermore, the N-CDs solution is blue (a) when exposed to UV light (365 nm) and appear light yellow (b) in natural light (inset in Fig. 3A). The emission spectra of N-CDs excited by different fluorescence wavelengths exhibited in Fig. 3B are recorded, and they obviously reflect that the emission wavelength of N-CDs shifts when excitation wavelengths change, which may be due to the surface state defects. The most intense emission of fluorescence 400 nm occurs at the excitation wavelength 320 nm. In addition, the N-CDs have a QY of approximately 7.65% in 0.1 M H2SO4 choosing quinine sulfate as standard reference.

The N-CDs maintain high level of fluorescence and absorbance stability in severe experiment condition. This is extremely important to practical application. First, the influence of pH on the fluorescence and absorbance was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S1A and Fig. S1B (Supporting information), when pH increased from 3.0 to 10.0, the intensity of fluorescence and absorbance of N-CDs were relatively stable. It can be seen from Fig. S1C and Fig. S1D, with the increase of the concentration of NaCl solution from 0.0 M to 1.0 M, high ionic strength had no influence on the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of N-CDs. The intensity of fluorescence (Fig. S1E) and absorbance (Fig. S1F) keep constant when N-CDs were stored at ambient temperature for 10 days. Therefore, the prepared N-CDs exhibit high optical stability under various experimental environments; this provides an outstanding approach for food detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode sensing mechanism of N-CDs for Fe3+ and FA
ce:section.ce:para: The possible dual-mode sensing mechanism applying N-CDs to Fe3+ and FA is illustrated in Fig. S2. Compared with other substances, only when Fe3+ (Fig. S2A) or FA (Fig. S2B) was added, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs rose sharply, this displayed obviously that the surface state was modified when N-CDs binding to Fe3+ or FA. When different concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. S2C) or FA (Fig. S2D) was added, the fluorescence lifetime of the system was almost unchanged, which also indicated the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs could be regarded as static quenching in the presence of Fe3+ or FA, this was attributed to N-CDs formed non-fluorescent complexes with Fe3+ or FA. To verify the mechanism deeply, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs were compared with the UV–vis absorption spectra. Fig. S2E (Fe3+) and Fig. S2F (FA) indicated that the two spectra overlapped, which indicated the occurrence of internal filtering effect (IFE) when Fe3+ or FA coexisted with N-CDs. In summary, we speculate that the dual-mode sensing mechanism of N-CDs for Fe3+ and FA is the synergistic effect which arose by the non-fluorescent complex and IFE [32,33].

It is widely known that high selectivity is necessary for a superior sensing system. That is why comparative experiments were conducted first. The fluorescence quenching efficiency F/F0 (F, F0 represent the intensity of fluorescence after adding the analyte and without adding anything respectively, Fig. S3A) and the changes of UV–vis absorbance at 283 nm (Fig. S3B) were recorded when 15 common metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Li+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, K+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) were added under the same experimental conditions into N-CDs at 540 μM. It showed clearly that only Fe3+ could lead to intense fluorescence quenching and enhance UV–vis absorption. In contrast, the other metal ions had almost no impact on quenching and absorption. These indicated that the synthesized N-CDs had excellent selectivity for Fe3+.

To achieve high sensitivity prior to the detection of Fe3+, some other experimental conditions were optimized, including pH, reaction time and N-CDs concentration. As illustrated in Fig. S3C, in the range of pH = 3.0 ∼ 10.0, both fluorescence intensity and ΔA (the absorbance difference between the presence and absence of the analyte) achieved maximum value when pH = 7.0. Thus, in the subsequent experiments pH = 7.0 was set. It can be seen from Fig. S3D, when Fe3+ was added, the response of fluorescence quenching and the increase of absorbance could be finished in 30 s. As a consequence, 30 s was selected to obtain more accurate experimental results. Furthermore, the concentration of N-CDs was another important factor to the experiment result. As shown in the figure, when the N-CDs concentration increasing, ΔA remained stable (Fig. S3E), meanwhile, F/F0 decreased at first and increased afterwards (Fig. S3F). According to these two situations above, 0.027 mg / mL was the optimal concentration of N-CDs.

The fluorescence at 400 nm and the absorbance at 283 nm were recorded by adding various concentrations of Fe3+ into N-CDs solution under the above conditions to evaluate the sensitivity of N-CDs-based dual-mode sensor. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, when Fe3+ concentration increased gradually, the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs decreased synchronously, which indicated clearly that Fe3+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs, and as demonstrated in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs had an ideal linear relationship (R2 = 0.9976) with Fe3+ concentration of 0.1–540 μΜ. The linear regression equation was F = -1.406 CFe3+ (μM) + 868.1, and the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was 0.02 μM. At the same time, the absorbance of N-CDs increased gradually alone with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 4C). The linear regression equation and LOD were A = 0.0021 CFe3+ (μM) + 0.2494 (R2 = 0.9975) (Fig. 4D) and 0.04 μM, respectively.

The prepared N-CDs have satisfactory biocompatibility and friendliness, and can be applied to study their potential application in the detection of biological small molecules. Similarly, fluorescence responses and UV–vis absorbance changes of N-CDs to various biological small molecules, such as L-glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), L-cysteine (Cys), dopamine (DA), nicotinic acid (NA), DL-homocysteine (Hcy), taurine (TA), ascorbic acid (AA), FA, had been studied under the same experimental conditions (165 μM). The results illustrated that only FA could significantly quench the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S4A) and enhance the absorbance of N-CDs (Fig. S4B), which indicated the prepared N-CDs had high selectivity to determine FA in aqueous solution, and could reach reaction equilibrium quickly (Fig. S4C). As shown in Fig. 5 A, when the concentration of FA increased, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched gradually, and there existed a nice linear relationship (R2 = 0.9912) between the concentration of FA of 0.1–165 μΜ and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs (Fig. 5B). The linear regression equation was F = − 4.262 CFA (μM) + 850.4 with LOD of 0.04 μM. In the meantime, the UV–vis absorbance at 283 nm of N-CDs enhanced dramatically with the incremental increase of FA concentration (Fig. 5C). The regression equation was A = 0.0073 CFA (μM) + 0.1996 (R2 = 0.9966) (Fig. 5D) with LOD of 0.03 μM.

In addition, the proposed strategy was compared with some other reported quantum dots detection methods for Fe3+ or FA. Table S1 and Table S2 showed that the proposed dual-mode detection method had comparable detection performance and significant advantages of easy synthesis, with no use of chemical reagents, green and pollution-free.

Celery, yolk and black sesame are rich in iron, so they were selected; and the standard addition recovery method was applied to test the actual performance of the prepared dual-mode sensor. The dried and ground samples of 6.0 g celery, 6.0 g yolk and 2.0 g black sesame were separately heated to ash, then transferred to muffle furnace and burned at 600 °C for 2 h. After cooling, 2 mL HCl solution was added to dissolve each ash, and then heated them to nearly dry, diluted to 3.00 mL with ultra-pure water. The fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorbance of sample solutions were measured by the proposed dual-mode method, and the standard addition recovery experiments were performed. As demonstrated in Table S3, the recoveries were 98.66% −102.46%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ 1.78%.

For the practical samples of FA, fresh vegetables and fruits were selected and pretreated as follows: 50.0 g vegetables (spinach, carrot) and fruits (peeled grape, orange) were cut into small pieces and extruded by a juicer, centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min, and filtered by membrane (0.22 µm) to remove insoluble impurities. Then, before diluting to 50.00 mL with ultra-pure water, 2 mL (2 mM) sodium hexametaphosphate was added to each of the four sample solutions to mask the possible presence of Fe3+. The FA content was determined immediately by the proposed dual-mode method. The results of the determination of FA were satisfactory (Table S4), specifically, the standard addition recovery ranged from 96.00 % to 102.00 % with the RSD ≤ 1.95 %.

For comparison, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) to Fe3+ (Table S3) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) to FA (Table S4) were applied to the samples, and the results indicated the proposed dual-mode method had no significant difference with these two methods. All of the results above showed clearly that the dual-mode sensor was sensitive and effective to determinate Fe3+ and FA in food field.

According to the discussion above, two logic gates are constructed successfully. The two inputs are Fe3+ (denoted by 1) and FA (denoted by 2), and their existence is set to “1” and their absence is set to “0”, respectively. As far as the outputs, “1” represents the significant fluorescence intensity and the obvious UV–vis absorption, while “0” represents the weak fluorescence intensity and the negligible UV–vis absorption. According to the results above, either input Fe3+ or input FA can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. Thus, as demonstrated in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence output is “0” when any input is present. In other words, the fluorescence output is “1” in the absence of any input. This result obeys the “NOR” logic gate of the Boolean operation. On the other hand, the input of either Fe3+ or FA can enhance the absorbance of the system. The result shown in Fig. S5B is the “OR” logic gate according to the Boolean operation, where the output is “1” when there are any inputs, and “0” otherwise. So, the “NOR” and “OR” logic gates are formed [34].

The synthesized N-CDs can be used as paper-based sensors on account of their outstanding fluorescence properties. For example, letters written by Fe3+ or FA solution on filter paper which soaked with N-CDs were illustrated under sunlight (Fig. S6A) and under ultraviolet (Fig. S6B). Nowadays, paper-based sensors are being used in the determination of small molecules, and may have wider applications in clinical diagnosis, biosensors and information transmission in the future [35].

From what has been discussed above, we may reasonably arrive at the conclusion that pine needles were used as a single precursor to construct a low expense, simple and feasible one-step hydrothermal synthesis for N-CDs; based on the excellent characteristics of the prepared N-CDs, a dual-mode sensor of fluorescence and absorbance was developed to detect Fe3+ and FA. At the same time, the two inputs “NOR” and “OR” logic gates were established. The proposed dual-mode method has high sensitivity and good selectivity and has been applied successfully to determinate Fe3+ and FA in practical samples with satisfactory results, which indicates that it has potential application and great prospect in food field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2019MB019, ZR2020MB075) and the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province, China (2019GSF111047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121891.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
